questions.questionText: Adult hemoglobin A is composed of:
questions.explanation: Adult hemoglobin A is a tetramer composed of two α-chains and two β-chains. Each subunit contains a heme group that can bind oxygen reversibly.

questions.questionText: Each α-chain of hemoglobin contains approximately:
questions.explanation: The α-chain of hemoglobin contains 141 amino acids. The β-chain contains 146 amino acids.

questions.questionText: Each β-chain of hemoglobin contains approximately:
questions.explanation: The β-chain contains 146 amino acids, while the α-chain contains 141 amino acids.

questions.questionText: Normal hemoglobin concentration in adult males is approximately:
questions.explanation: The normal hemoglobin range listed for males is 14–18 g/dL.

questions.questionText: Normal hemoglobin concentration in adult females is approximately:
questions.explanation: The normal hemoglobin range listed for females is 12–16 g/dL.

questions.questionText: Hemoglobin is responsible for the red color of blood mainly because it contains:
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin contains heme, an iron-containing porphyrin pigment responsible for the red color of blood.

questions.questionText: Oxyhemoglobin is formed when hemoglobin binds:
questions.explanation: Oxyhemoglobin is formed when hemoglobin binds oxygen reversibly: Hb + 4O₂ ⇌ Hb·4O₂.

questions.questionText: In functional oxyhemoglobin, iron is mainly in the:
questions.explanation: Functional hemoglobin binds oxygen through ferrous iron Fe²⁺. Oxidation to Fe³⁺ forms methemoglobin, which cannot bind oxygen normally.

questions.questionText: Oxygen binding to hemoglobin is:
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin binds oxygen reversibly. This allows oxygen loading in the lungs and unloading in peripheral tissues.

questions.questionText: In the lungs, hemoglobin tends to:
questions.explanation: In the lungs, oxygen tension is high, so hemoglobin binds oxygen and forms oxyhemoglobin.

questions.questionText: In tissues, hemoglobin tends to:
questions.explanation: In tissues, oxygen tension is low, so hemoglobin releases oxygen for cellular metabolism.

questions.questionText: The oxygen dissociation curve of hemoglobin is:
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin has a sigmoidal oxygen dissociation curve because its subunits cooperate during oxygen binding.

questions.questionText: Cooperative binding means that binding of oxygen to one heme group:
options: Increases oxygen affinity of the remaining heme groups
Prevents oxygen binding to other heme groups
questions.explanation: In hemoglobin, oxygen binding to one subunit increases the affinity of the remaining subunits for oxygen.

questions.questionText: The Bohr effect describes the effect of changes in:
options: pH and pCO₂ on oxygen binding
Glucose and insulin on oxygen binding
DNA mutation on heme synthesis only
questions.explanation: The Bohr effect describes how increased CO₂ and H⁺ decrease hemoglobin’s oxygen affinity, promoting oxygen release in tissues.

questions.questionText: Increased pCO₂ and increased H⁺ cause hemoglobin oxygen affinity to:
questions.explanation: Increased CO₂ and H⁺ lower pH and decrease hemoglobin’s oxygen affinity, helping tissues receive oxygen.

questions.questionText: Decreased pCO₂ and decreased H⁺ cause hemoglobin oxygen affinity to:
questions.explanation: Lower CO₂ and lower H⁺ increase pH, increasing hemoglobin affinity for oxygen. This favors oxygen loading in lungs.

questions.questionText: 2,3-BPG mainly:
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG decreases hemoglobin’s oxygen affinity, helping oxygen unloading in tissues.

questions.questionText: 2,3-BPG binds mainly to:
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG binds to deoxyhemoglobin, not oxyhemoglobin.

questions.questionText: The presence of 2,3-BPG shifts the oxygen dissociation curve:
options: It does not affect the curve
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG decreases oxygen affinity and shifts the curve to the right, enhancing oxygen release.

questions.questionText: Removal of 2,3-BPG from hemoglobin causes:
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin without 2,3-BPG has abnormally high oxygen affinity and releases oxygen poorly.

questions.questionText: 2,3-BPG is synthesized from an intermediate of:
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG is synthesized from an intermediate of the glycolytic pathway in red blood cells.

questions.questionText: 2,3-BPG is abundant in:
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG is the most abundant organic phosphate in red blood cells, with a concentration approximately equal to that of hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: Chronic hypoxia tends to:
questions.explanation: In chronic hypoxia, red blood cell 2,3-BPG increases, helping hemoglobin release more oxygen to tissues.

questions.questionText: Chronic anemia tends to:
questions.explanation: In chronic anemia, fewer RBCs are available to supply oxygen, so 2,3-BPG rises to promote oxygen unloading.

questions.questionText: Most carbon dioxide produced in metabolism is transported as:
questions.explanation: Most CO₂, about 85%, is hydrated and transported as bicarbonate ion.

questions.questionText: Carbaminohemoglobin is formed when CO₂ binds to:
questions.explanation: Some CO₂ is carried as carbamate bound to the uncharged α-amino groups of hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: Carboxyhemoglobin is formed when hemoglobin binds:
questions.explanation: Carboxyhemoglobin forms when hemoglobin binds carbon monoxide (CO).

questions.questionText: Hemoglobin affinity for CO is approximately how many times its affinity for oxygen?
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin affinity for carbon monoxide is about 210 times its affinity for oxygen.

questions.questionText: Methemoglobin contains iron in the:
questions.explanation: Methemoglobin contains oxidized iron, Fe³⁺.

questions.questionText: Normal hemoglobin contains iron mainly in the:
questions.explanation: Normal oxygen-binding hemoglobin contains ferrous iron Fe²⁺.

questions.questionText: Methemoglobin is normally reduced back to hemoglobin by:
questions.explanation: Methemoglobin reductase reduces Fe³⁺ methemoglobin back to Fe²⁺ hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: Cyanide toxicity mainly inhibits:
options: Respiratory enzymes such as cytochrome oxidase
questions.explanation: Cyanide binds ferric cytochrome enzymes and inhibits respiration.

questions.questionText: Sodium nitrite helps in cyanide poisoning by forming:
questions.explanation: Sodium nitrite is a mild oxidizing agent that converts hemoglobin to methemoglobin. Methemoglobin can bind cyanide to form non-toxic cyanomethemoglobin.

questions.questionText: Fetal hemoglobin is composed of:
questions.explanation: Fetal hemoglobin, HbF, is composed of two α-chains and two γ-chains.

questions.questionText: HbA2 is composed of:
questions.explanation: HbA2 is a minor adult hemoglobin composed of two α-chains and two δ-chains.

questions.questionText: HbA1c is important clinically because it increases in:
questions.explanation: HbA1c is glycosylated hemoglobin. It increases in diabetes because hemoglobin is exposed to higher glucose concentrations during the RBC lifespan.

questions.questionText: Why is the oxygen dissociation curve of hemoglobin sigmoidal?
options: Because oxygen binding by one subunit increases affinity of the remaining subunits
Because hemoglobin has only one heme group
questions.explanation: The sigmoidal curve reflects cooperative binding. Hemoglobin has four subunits, and oxygen binding to one subunit increases the oxygen affinity of the others.

questions.questionText: Which feature of hemoglobin allows cooperative oxygen binding?
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin has four subunits. Cooperation between these subunits produces the sigmoidal oxygen dissociation curve.

questions.questionText: A right shift of the oxygen dissociation curve indicates:
options: Decreased oxygen affinity and increased oxygen unloading
Increased oxygen affinity and decreased oxygen unloading
questions.explanation: A right shift means hemoglobin holds oxygen less tightly, so it releases oxygen more efficiently to tissues.

questions.questionText: A left shift of the oxygen dissociation curve indicates:
options: Increased oxygen affinity and decreased oxygen unloading
Decreased oxygen affinity and increased oxygen unloading
questions.explanation: A left shift means hemoglobin binds oxygen more tightly, making oxygen unloading to tissues more difficult.

questions.questionText: During intense exercise, lactic acid production would most likely:
questions.explanation: Lactic acid increases H⁺ concentration and lowers pH. This decreases hemoglobin oxygen affinity and promotes oxygen release to active tissues.

questions.questionText: In metabolically active tissues, pCO₂ is high. This causes:
options: Increased H⁺ formation and decreased Hb oxygen affinity
Decreased H⁺ formation and increased Hb oxygen affinity
Complete oxidation of heme to bilirubin
questions.explanation: CO₂ combines with water to form carbonic acid, which dissociates into bicarbonate and H⁺. Increased H⁺ lowers pH and decreases hemoglobin oxygen affinity.

questions.questionText: In alveolar capillaries of the lungs, pCO₂ is low. This favors:
options: Increased oxygen affinity and oxygen loading
Decreased oxygen affinity and oxygen release
questions.explanation: Low CO₂ decreases H⁺ formation, raises pH, and increases oxygen affinity. This favors oxygen loading in the lungs.

questions.questionText: Which combination favors oxygen unloading in tissues?
options: Increased CO₂, increased H⁺, decreased pH
Decreased CO₂, decreased H⁺, increased pH
questions.explanation: High CO₂ and H⁺ decrease hemoglobin oxygen affinity. This promotes oxygen unloading in tissues.

questions.questionText: Which combination favors oxygen loading in the lungs?
options: Decreased CO₂, decreased H⁺, increased pH
Increased CO₂, increased H⁺, decreased pH
questions.explanation: Low CO₂ and H⁺ increase pH and hemoglobin affinity for oxygen, promoting oxygen loading in the lungs.

questions.questionText: 2,3-BPG decreases oxygen affinity because it binds:
options: Deoxyhemoglobin and stabilizes the low-affinity form
Oxyhemoglobin and stabilizes the high-affinity form
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG binds deoxyhemoglobin and stabilizes its oxygen-releasing form, decreasing oxygen affinity.

questions.questionText: The binding site of 2,3-BPG is located:
options: In the central pocket formed by the two β-globin chains
questions.explanation: One molecule of 2,3-BPG binds in the central pocket of deoxyhemoglobin formed by the two β-chains.

questions.questionText: 2,3-BPG binds to hemoglobin through interactions between:
options: Negatively charged phosphate groups and positively charged amino acids
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG has negatively charged phosphate groups that form ionic bonds with positively charged amino acids in the β-chain pocket.

questions.questionText: 2,3-BPG is expelled from hemoglobin when:
questions.explanation: When hemoglobin binds oxygen, 2,3-BPG is expelled from its central pocket.

questions.questionText: Why does increased 2,3-BPG help in chronic anemia?
options: It helps the remaining hemoglobin unload oxygen more efficiently
It increases the number of red cells immediately
questions.explanation: In anemia, oxygen delivery is reduced. Increased 2,3-BPG decreases hemoglobin affinity and promotes oxygen release to tissues.

questions.questionText: Why does increased 2,3-BPG help at high altitude?
options: It improves oxygen unloading in tissues when oxygen supply is limited
It prevents oxygen binding in the lungs completely
questions.explanation: At high altitude, oxygen loading is more difficult. Increased 2,3-BPG helps by promoting tissue oxygen unloading.

questions.questionText: Stored blood with low 2,3-BPG has:
questions.explanation: Loss of 2,3-BPG during storage causes hemoglobin to bind oxygen too tightly, reducing oxygen unloading.

questions.questionText: Stored blood deficient in 2,3-BPG may act as:
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin deficient in 2,3-BPG binds oxygen strongly and releases it poorly, acting like an oxygen trap.

questions.questionText: Transfused red cells usually restore depleted 2,3-BPG within:
questions.explanation: Transfused red blood cells can restore their 2,3-BPG stores within 24–48 hours.

questions.questionText: Adding inosine to stored blood helps prevent decreased 2,3-BPG because inosine can enter pathways producing:
questions.explanation: Inosine can enter RBC metabolism through ribose-related pathways, supporting 2,3-BPG formation.

questions.questionText: Most CO₂ is transported as bicarbonate because CO₂ reacts with water to form:
questions.explanation: CO₂ + H₂O forms carbonic acid, which dissociates into bicarbonate and H⁺. Most CO₂ is transported as bicarbonate.

questions.questionText: Carbaminohemoglobin formation involves CO₂ binding to:
questions.explanation: CO₂ forms carbaminohemoglobin by binding to uncharged amino groups of hemoglobin, not to heme iron.

questions.questionText: Which statement correctly distinguishes carbaminohemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin?
options: Carbaminohemoglobin contains CO₂; carboxyhemoglobin contains CO
Carbaminohemoglobin contains CO; carboxyhemoglobin contains CO₂
questions.explanation: Carbaminohemoglobin is Hb-bound CO₂. Carboxyhemoglobin is Hb-bound carbon monoxide.

questions.questionText: Carbon monoxide poisoning is dangerous mainly because CO:
options: Binds hemoglobin much more strongly than oxygen
questions.explanation: CO has about 210 times the affinity of oxygen for hemoglobin, reducing oxygen transport.

questions.questionText: In carboxyhemoglobin formation, CO displaces:
questions.explanation: The reaction can be represented as Hb·4O₂ + 4CO → Hb·4CO + 4O₂, showing CO displaces oxygen.

questions.questionText: Methemoglobin is unable to bind oxygen properly because:
options: Iron is oxidized from Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺
Globin chains are converted into bilirubin
questions.explanation: Methemoglobin contains ferric iron Fe³⁺, which cannot bind oxygen normally.

questions.questionText: Which statement about methemoglobin is NOT true?
options: Its iron is in the ferrous state
It can be reduced back to hemoglobin by methemoglobin reductase
It cannot combine with oxygen normally
questions.explanation: This is the false statement. Methemoglobin contains ferric Fe³⁺, not ferrous Fe²⁺.

questions.questionText: Hydrogen peroxide or free radicals can convert hemoglobin into:
questions.explanation: Oxidants such as hydrogen peroxide and free radicals can oxidize Fe²⁺ hemoglobin to Fe³⁺ methemoglobin.

questions.questionText: Cyanide binds strongly to:
questions.explanation: Cyanide binds ferric iron in cytochrome oxidase and inhibits cellular respiration.

questions.questionText: The purpose of giving sodium nitrite in cyanide poisoning is to:
options: Produce methemoglobin that can bind cyanide
questions.explanation: Sodium nitrite oxidizes some hemoglobin to methemoglobin. Methemoglobin binds cyanide to form cyanomethemoglobin, reducing cyanide toxicity.

questions.questionText: Cyanomethemoglobin is considered:
options: Non-toxic compared with free cyanide binding respiratory enzymes
questions.explanation: Cyanide bound to methemoglobin as cyanomethemoglobin is relatively non-toxic compared with cyanide inhibiting cytochrome oxidase.

questions.questionText: Fetal hemoglobin has higher oxygen affinity than adult hemoglobin mainly because:
questions.explanation: HbF has γ-chains instead of β-chains, and these γ-chains bind 2,3-BPG weakly. Less 2,3-BPG effect means higher oxygen affinity.

questions.questionText: The γ-chains of HbF bind 2,3-BPG weakly because they:
options: Lack some positively charged amino acids present in β-chains
Contain only heme and no protein
questions.explanation: The γ-chains lack some positively charged residues needed for strong 2,3-BPG binding.

questions.questionText: The higher oxygen affinity of HbF helps:
options: Transfer oxygen from maternal blood to fetal blood
questions.explanation: HbF’s higher oxygen affinity facilitates oxygen transfer across the placenta from the mother to the fetus.

questions.questionText: HbA synthesis begins significantly at about:
questions.explanation: HbA synthesis starts in bone marrow at about the eighth month of pregnancy and gradually replaces HbF.

questions.questionText: HbF accounts for about what percentage of total hemoglobin in the last months of fetal life?
questions.explanation: HbF accounts for about 60% of total hemoglobin in the erythrocytes during the last months of fetal life.

questions.questionText: HbA2 first appears:
options: In the first week after conception
questions.explanation: HbA2 appears about twelve weeks after birth.

questions.questionText: HbA2 ultimately constitutes approximately:
questions.explanation: HbA2 is a minor adult hemoglobin and constitutes about 2% of total hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: HbA1c is produced by:
options: Carbon dioxide binding to heme iron
questions.explanation: HbA1c forms by slow, nonenzymatic glycosylation of HbA.

questions.questionText: HbA1c glucose residues attach mainly to:
questions.explanation: HbA1c has glucose residues attached predominantly to the NH₂ groups of N-terminal valines of β-globin chains.

questions.questionText: HbA1c reflects blood glucose exposure over the RBC lifespan because RBCs live approximately:
questions.explanation: Red blood cells live about 120 days, so HbA1c reflects glycemic exposure over a prolonged period.

questions.questionText: Which hemoglobin appears earliest in embryonic life according to the file?
questions.explanation: In the first few weeks after conception, embryonic hemoglobin Hb Gower 1 is synthesized.

questions.questionText: Hb Gower 1 is composed of:
questions.explanation: Hb Gower 1 is composed of two zeta chains and two epsilon chains, written ζ₂ε₂.

questions.questionText: Fetal liver begins synthesizing mainly:
questions.explanation: Within a few weeks, the fetal liver begins to synthesize HbF.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes HbF from HbA?
options: HbF is α₂γ₂ and binds 2,3-BPG weakly; HbA is α₂β₂
HbF is α₂β₂ and binds 2,3-BPG strongly
questions.explanation: HbF contains γ-chains and binds 2,3-BPG weakly, giving it higher oxygen affinity. HbA contains β-chains.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes HbA2 from HbA?
options: HbA2 is α₂δ₂, while HbA is α₂β₂
HbA2 is α₂γ₂, while HbA is α₂δ₂
HbA2 contains zeta and epsilon chains
questions.explanation: HbA2 contains δ-chains instead of β-chains. HbA is α₂β₂; HbA2 is α₂δ₂.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes HbA1c from HbA?
questions.explanation: HbA1c is the glycosylated form of HbA, with glucose residues attached nonenzymatically.

questions.questionText: In diabetes mellitus, HbA1c increases because:
options: HbA is exposed to higher glucose concentrations during RBC lifespan
questions.explanation: Higher blood glucose increases nonenzymatic glycosylation of hemoglobin, increasing HbA1c.

questions.questionText: A hemoglobin variant with weak 2,3-BPG binding and high oxygen affinity is most likely:
questions.explanation: HbF binds 2,3-BPG weakly due to its γ-chains, causing higher oxygen affinity.

questions.questionText: Which factor helps HbF take oxygen from maternal HbA?
questions.explanation: HbF’s higher affinity allows it to bind oxygen at the lower oxygen tensions present in placental circulation.

questions.questionText: Which is the most accurate relationship between 2,3-BPG and HbF?
options: HbF weakly binds 2,3-BPG, so its oxygen affinity is high
HbF strongly binds 2,3-BPG, so its oxygen affinity is low
questions.explanation: Weak 2,3-BPG binding means less reduction in oxygen affinity, so HbF holds oxygen more strongly.

questions.questionText: Which statement about 2,3-BPG in stored blood is most clinically relevant?
options: Low 2,3-BPG may reduce oxygen unloading after transfusion
Low 2,3-BPG increases carbon dioxide transport only
questions.explanation: Stored blood may lose 2,3-BPG, causing hemoglobin to bind oxygen too tightly and fail to unload oxygen properly.

questions.questionText: Which condition would shift the oxygen dissociation curve to the right?
questions.explanation: Increased 2,3-BPG decreases oxygen affinity and shifts the curve to the right.

questions.questionText: Which condition would shift the oxygen dissociation curve to the left?
questions.explanation: Decreased 2,3-BPG increases hemoglobin oxygen affinity and shifts the curve left.

questions.questionText: Which factor promotes oxygen release in tissues?
questions.explanation: Increased H⁺ decreases pH, decreases hemoglobin oxygen affinity, and promotes oxygen release.

questions.questionText: Which factor promotes oxygen loading in lungs?
questions.explanation: In the lungs, CO₂ is released into expired air. Lower CO₂ leads to decreased H⁺ and increased oxygen affinity.

questions.questionText: Which statement best links carbonic anhydrase activity to the Bohr effect?
options: CO₂ hydration generates H⁺, which lowers pH and decreases Hb oxygen affinity
Carbonic anhydrase converts hemoglobin into bilirubin
questions.explanation: CO₂ + H₂O forms H₂CO₃, which dissociates into HCO₃⁻ and H⁺. The H⁺ contributes to the Bohr effect.

questions.questionText: Which tissue condition most strongly favors oxygen unloading?
options: High CO₂ and high lactic acid
questions.explanation: High CO₂ and lactic acid increase H⁺, decrease pH, and reduce hemoglobin oxygen affinity.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes hemoglobin in the absence of 2,3-BPG?
options: It has high oxygen affinity and releases oxygen poorly
It has low oxygen affinity and releases oxygen easily
It cannot bind oxygen at all
questions.explanation: Without 2,3-BPG, hemoglobin holds oxygen too tightly, making tissue delivery inefficient.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes why 2,3-BPG is essential for normal oxygen transport?
options: It prevents hemoglobin from trapping oxygen by promoting unloading in tissues
It prevents oxygen binding in lungs completely
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG lowers hemoglobin oxygen affinity enough to allow effective oxygen unloading in tissues.

questions.questionText: A patient receives a large transfusion of old stored blood. The main concern related to 2,3-BPG is:
options: Poor oxygen unloading due to low 2,3-BPG
Excess oxygen unloading due to high 2,3-BPG
questions.explanation: Old stored blood may have depleted 2,3-BPG. This increases hemoglobin oxygen affinity and reduces oxygen unloading.

questions.questionText: A patient at high altitude develops increased RBC 2,3-BPG. Which change is expected?
options: Right shift of oxygen dissociation curve and improved tissue oxygen release
Left shift and impaired oxygen release
questions.explanation: High altitude causes chronic hypoxia, increasing 2,3-BPG. This shifts the curve right and improves oxygen unloading in tissues.

questions.questionText: A patient with chronic anemia has increased 2,3-BPG. What is the adaptive benefit?
options: Remaining hemoglobin releases oxygen more efficiently
questions.explanation: In anemia, oxygen-carrying capacity is reduced. Increased 2,3-BPG helps compensate by improving oxygen unloading.

questions.questionText: A blood sample stored in acid-citrate-dextrose has low 2,3-BPG. Which statement is most likely?
options: Hemoglobin affinity for oxygen is abnormally high
Hemoglobin affinity for oxygen is abnormally low
Hemoglobin cannot bind oxygen at all
questions.explanation: Low 2,3-BPG increases oxygen affinity, so stored blood may fail to unload oxygen effectively.

questions.questionText: A neonate’s HbF has higher oxygen affinity than maternal HbA. The most direct biochemical reason is:
options: Oxidation of HbF iron to Fe³⁺
questions.explanation: HbF has γ-chains that lack some positively charged residues required for strong 2,3-BPG binding. Therefore, HbF has higher oxygen affinity.

questions.questionText: If a mutation in γ-globin increased positive residues in the 2,3-BPG pocket, the likely effect would be:
options: Stronger 2,3-BPG binding and lower oxygen affinity
Weaker 2,3-BPG binding and higher oxygen affinity
No possible effect on oxygen binding
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG binds positively charged residues through ionic interactions. More positive residues could strengthen binding, decreasing oxygen affinity.

questions.questionText: Which scenario best demonstrates the Bohr effect?
options: Active muscle produces CO₂ and H⁺, causing hemoglobin to release oxygen
Stored blood loses 2,3-BPG and releases oxygen better
Cyanide binds methemoglobin and forms HbA1c
HbA2 appears after birth due to glucose exposure
questions.explanation: The Bohr effect links increased CO₂/H⁺ with reduced hemoglobin oxygen affinity and increased oxygen release.

questions.questionText: A patient with severe lactic acidosis would be expected to have:
options: Complete conversion of Hb to HbA2
questions.explanation: Lactic acidosis increases H⁺ and lowers pH. This decreases hemoglobin oxygen affinity, promoting oxygen release.

questions.questionText: Which combination most strongly produces a left shift?
questions.explanation: Low CO₂/H⁺ increases oxygen affinity, and low 2,3-BPG also increases oxygen affinity. Together they shift the curve left.

questions.questionText: Which combination most strongly produces a right shift?
questions.explanation: High CO₂/H⁺ and high 2,3-BPG all decrease oxygen affinity, promoting a right shift and oxygen unloading.

questions.questionText: A patient has CO poisoning. Which mechanism best explains tissue hypoxia?
options: CO binds hemoglobin with much higher affinity than oxygen, reducing oxygen transport
questions.explanation: CO binds hemoglobin about 210 times more strongly than oxygen, limiting oxygen transport and causing tissue hypoxia.

questions.questionText: Which statement distinguishes carboxyhemoglobin from methemoglobin?
options: Carboxyhemoglobin contains CO-bound Hb; methemoglobin contains Fe³⁺ Hb
Carboxyhemoglobin contains Fe³⁺; methemoglobin contains CO₂
questions.explanation: Carboxyhemoglobin forms when CO binds hemoglobin. Methemoglobin forms when iron is oxidized to Fe³⁺.

questions.questionText: Which statement distinguishes carbaminohemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin?
options: Carbaminohemoglobin carries CO₂ on amino groups; carboxyhemoglobin carries CO
Carbaminohemoglobin carries CO; carboxyhemoglobin carries CO₂
questions.explanation: Carbaminohemoglobin is CO₂ bound to hemoglobin amino groups. Carboxyhemoglobin is carbon monoxide bound to hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: A patient has increased oxidant exposure converting Hb Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺. Which form increases?
questions.explanation: Oxidation of hemoglobin iron from Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ produces methemoglobin.

questions.questionText: In cyanide poisoning, sodium nitrite is useful because:
options: It creates methemoglobin that diverts cyanide away from cytochrome oxidase
It reduces all methemoglobin to hemoglobin immediately
It increases oxygen affinity of HbF
questions.explanation: Sodium nitrite forms methemoglobin. Cyanide binds methemoglobin to form cyanomethemoglobin, reducing inhibition of respiratory enzymes.

questions.questionText: Why is cyanide binding to cytochrome oxidase more dangerous than cyanide binding to methemoglobin?
options: Cytochrome oxidase inhibition blocks cellular respiration
Methemoglobin binding cyanide increases respiration inhibition
Cyanomethemoglobin is the toxic respiratory enzyme
questions.explanation: Cyanide inhibits cytochrome oxidase, blocking mitochondrial respiration. Binding cyanide to methemoglobin forms a less toxic complex.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why HbA1c reflects long-term glycemic status?
options: It forms slowly and nonenzymatically during the 120-day RBC lifespan
It forms instantly after one glucose meal
It is produced only in fetal liver
questions.explanation: HbA1c accumulates over the RBC lifespan as hemoglobin is exposed to glucose, making it useful for monitoring diabetes.

questions.questionText: A diabetic patient has high HbA1c. Which molecular event is increased?
options: Nonenzymatic glycosylation of N-terminal valines of β-chains
questions.explanation: HbA1c forms by glucose attaching to the N-terminal valines of β-globin chains in HbA.

questions.questionText: A fetus can extract oxygen from maternal blood because HbF:
options: Has higher oxygen affinity due to weaker 2,3-BPG binding
Has lower oxygen affinity due to stronger 2,3-BPG binding
questions.explanation: HbF binds 2,3-BPG weakly, so its oxygen affinity is higher than HbA, allowing placental oxygen transfer.

questions.questionText: A hemoglobin with α₂δ₂ composition is identified. Which form is it?
questions.explanation: HbA2 is α₂δ₂ and makes up about 2% of adult hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: A hemoglobin with α₂γ₂ composition is identified. Which form is it?
questions.explanation: HbF consists of two α-chains and two γ-chains.

questions.questionText: A hemoglobin with α₂β₂ composition is identified. Which form is it?
questions.explanation: Adult hemoglobin A is α₂β₂.

questions.questionText: A hemoglobin with ζ₂ε₂ composition appears early in development. Which form is it?
questions.explanation: Embryonic Hb Gower 1 is composed of two ζ-chains and two ε-chains.

questions.questionText: Which option best integrates 2,3-BPG, HbF, and oxygen affinity?
options: Less 2,3-BPG binding means higher oxygen affinity, explaining HbF’s oxygen transfer advantage
More 2,3-BPG binding means higher oxygen affinity
2,3-BPG has no effect on HbF or HbA
HbF has low oxygen affinity because it binds 2,3-BPG strongly
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG lowers oxygen affinity. Since HbF binds 2,3-BPG weakly, HbF maintains higher oxygen affinity and can draw oxygen from maternal HbA.

questions.questionText: Which option best integrates oxygen transport regulation?
options: CO₂/H⁺ and 2,3-BPG decrease oxygen affinity, while HbF and low 2,3-BPG increase oxygen affinity
HbF has lower oxygen affinity because it binds 2,3-BPG strongly
Stored blood with low 2,3-BPG releases oxygen better
questions.explanation: High CO₂/H⁺ and high 2,3-BPG promote oxygen release by decreasing affinity. HbF and low 2,3-BPG increase oxygen affinity, making hemoglobin hold oxygen more tightly.
